metadata.name: IS→UK Icelandic Seafood Export Corridor — W4M-WWG Instance Data
metadata.domain: International Seafood Trade / Cold Chain Logistics
metadata.description: Complete graph of the Grindavik/Akureyri fish processing → Port of Reykjavik → North Atlantic → Port of Immingham → Humber BCP → Grimsby/WWG Cold Store corridor for Icelandic cod, haddock and salmon (fresh/chilled fillets and frozen portions). Includes 7 chain nodes, 5 legs, 8 parties, 6 compliance gates, 10 trade documents, 6 bottlenecks with risk assessments and net impact, 3 hypotheses, 3 scenarios, 4 cascade effects, 3 predictive patterns, 1 example shipment.

examples.name: Icelandic Seafood → UK Retail & Foodservice (WWG)
examples.description: Fresh/chilled and frozen Icelandic cod, haddock and salmon from MAST-accredited processing facilities in Grindavik to UK distribution via Port of Reykjavik and Port of Immingham. Short North Atlantic corridor (4-7 days total transit). Fresh fillets: 10-14 day product life, critical shelf-life pressure. Frozen portions: 12-18 month shelf life, lower time pressure. EEA/EFTA bilateral trade — no tariff barriers but UK BTOM SPS requirements apply post-Brexit. CIF Immingham terms.
examples.commodity: Icelandic seafood (cod, haddock, salmon — fresh/chilled fillets and frozen portions)
examples.regulatoryRegime: EEA/EFTA Agreement; Iceland Fisheries Management Act; UK BTOM 2024; MAST Export Requirements
examples.annualVolume: 3,800 tonnes (180 × reefer containers + 50 air freight shipments/year)

examples.name: Grindavik Fish Processing Facility
examples.capabilities: filleting, skinning, pin-boning, vacuum-pack, blast-freeze, IQF, MAST-accredited, BRC-certified, MSC Chain of Custody

examples.name: Reykjavik Cold Storage (Sundahofn Export Terminal)
examples.capabilities: -2C to -25C holding, reefer container pre-loading, MAST inspection staging, container pre-cool

examples.name: Port of Reykjavik — Sundahofn Container Terminal
examples.capabilities: reefer-plug, container-yard, customs-clearance, MAST-inspection, weighbridge, cold-store-adjacent

examples.name: North Atlantic Ocean Transit
examples.location: North Atlantic — Reykjavik to Immingham direct

examples.name: Port of Immingham — Humber Container Terminal
Humber Border Control Post (BCP)
examples.operator: APHA (Animal and Plant Health Agency) / North East Lincolnshire Port Health Authority
examples.capabilities: SPS-inspection, document-verification, identity-check, physical-inspection, laboratory-sampling, seafood-specialist

examples.name: Grimsby / WWG Cold Store & Distribution Centre
examples.location: Grimsby Fish Dock / Europarc, Great Grimsby, Lincolnshire
examples.capabilities: -2C to -25C holding, multi-temp, pick-and-pack, portion-packing, retail-ready, foodservice-prep, stock-rotation (FIFO), onward-distribution-national

examples.route: Reykjavik then North Atlantic then Immingham

examples.carrier: DFDS Logistics UK / Local Humber haulier
examples.distance: 15 km (Immingham BCP to Grimsby Fish Dock)

examples.name: Thorbjorn hf (Icelandic Fish Processors)
examples.role: Fish processor and exporter — filleting, packaging, freezing, export documentation
examples.responsibilities: Product quality, MAST compliance, health certificate application, container loading, shelf-life guarantee, MSC Chain of Custody traceability
examples.riskOwnership: Product quality to FOB. Cold chain to FCA Grindavik. Regulatory compliance (IS side).
examples.licences: MAST Registered Establishment IS-0847, BRC Grade AA, MSC-C-55721

examples.name: WWG Seafood Imports Ltd
examples.role: UK importer of record — customs clearance, IPAFFS notification, BCP liaison, cold storage, national distribution
examples.responsibilities: UK import compliance, IPAFFS pre-notification, CHED-P submission, duty payment, cold storage, retail and foodservice delivery, portion-packing
examples.riskOwnership: All risk from CIF Immingham. UK regulatory compliance. Storage and distribution costs.
examples.licences: HMRC EORI GB-741829365000, IPAFFS Registered Importer, DEFRA Approved, BRC Storage & Distribution

examples.name: Samskip hf
examples.role: Ocean carrier — Reykjavik to Immingham, weekly North Atlantic service
examples.responsibilities: Vessel operation, reefer container supply, temperature monitoring at sea, on-time arrival, B/L issuance, weather routing
examples.riskOwnership: Carrier liability under Hague-Visby Rules. Reefer machinery breakdown. Weather-related delay (force majeure).

examples.name: Tollvörugaesla Customs Services (IS Broker)
examples.role: Icelandic customs broker — export declaration, tariff classification, EEA origin documentation
examples.responsibilities: Export declaration lodgement, EEA Certificate of Origin, fisheries quota verification, MAST liaison
examples.riskOwnership: Declaration accuracy. Penalty liability for misclassification.

examples.name: KGH Customs UK Ltd
examples.role: UK customs broker — import declaration, BTOM compliance, duty/VAT, seafood specialist
examples.responsibilities: Import customs declaration (CDS), tariff classification, EEA preference claim, duty payment, BTOM compliance

examples.name: MAST (Icelandic Food and Veterinary Authority)
examples.role: Icelandic export regulatory authority — fish inspection, health certification, establishment audit, fisheries compliance
examples.responsibilities: Processing facility inspection, health certificate issuance, export permit, establishment registration, residue and contaminant testing, catch documentation scheme verification
examples.riskOwnership: Regulatory authority — sets the rules. Non-negotiable.
examples.contactSLA: Government SLA (2-3 business days standard, 24 hours urgent)

examples.name: APHA (Animal & Plant Health Agency)
examples.role: UK border SPS authority — import inspection, health certificate verification, physical checks for fishery products
examples.responsibilities: CHED-P verification, identity checks, physical inspection (random and risk-based), laboratory sampling, import authorisation
examples.riskOwnership: Regulatory authority — can reject, detain, or destroy non-compliant consignments.

examples.name: Samskip Domestic Logistics ehf
examples.role: Domestic reefer haulage — Grindavik processing to Port of Reykjavik
examples.responsibilities: Reefer truck operation, temperature maintenance, GPS tracking, timely delivery to port cut-off

examples.name: DFDS Logistics UK Ltd
examples.role: UK inland reefer haulage — Immingham BCP to Grimsby cold store and onwards
examples.responsibilities: Container collection from BCP, reefer temperature maintenance, delivery to Grimsby, empty container return to Immingham
examples.riskOwnership: In-transit temperature UK side. Delay to cold store.

examples.name: MAST Health Certificate & Export Permit
examples.commonFailureReasons: Heavy metal test positive (mercury in large cod), catch documentation mismatch, establishment audit non-conformance, quota verification failure

examples.name: Icelandic Export Customs Clearance
examples.commonFailureReasons: Quota export limit reached, tariff classification dispute, incomplete declaration

examples.name: IPAFFS Pre-Notification (UK)
examples.avgDuration: Must be submitted 1-4 days before arrival
examples.commonFailureReasons: Late submission, health certificate number mismatch, commodity code error (fish species confusion), missing CHED-P reference

examples.name: BCP Document & Identity Check
examples.authority: North East Lincolnshire Port Health Authority / APHA
examples.commonFailureReasons: Health cert / CHED-P mismatch, species declaration discrepancy, seal number inconsistency, temperature log gaps

examples.name: BCP Physical / SPS Inspection (Fishery Products)
examples.commonFailureReasons: Temperature excursion evidence, histamine levels in fish (scombroid), visual spoilage, off-odour, parasites (Anisakis), packaging damage

examples.name: UK Import Customs Clearance (CDS)
examples.commonFailureReasons: Incorrect tariff code (fish species classification), EEA preference claim rejected, duty underpayment

examples.issuingAuthority: Iceland Chamber of Commerce / Exporter self-declaration (EEA origin)

examples._section: === BOTTLENECKS (The Tight Spots) with Risk + Net Impact ===

examples.name: North Atlantic Weather — Oct-Mar Storm Season
examples.description: North Atlantic storms Oct-Mar cause vessel delays, route diversions, and occasional port closure at both Reykjavik and Immingham. Force 10+ gales common. Vessels may shelter in Faroe Islands or heave-to. 4-8 significant storm events per winter season. Short corridor means even 1-2 day delay is proportionally severe for fresh fish shelf life.
examples.seasonality: Oct-Mar peak (North Atlantic winter storm season)
examples.rootCause: North Atlantic low-pressure systems; Icelandic weather variability; short-corridor sensitivity
examples.mitigationStrategy: Weather routing; flexible sailing schedule; air freight backup for urgent fresh fish; frozen buffer stock
examples.workaroundDescription: Switch critical fresh fish to air freight (Keflavik–Humberside or Keflavik–East Midlands) at 4x sea freight cost; convert fresh orders to frozen if delay >2 days forecast

examples.name: Icelandic Fishing Quota Restrictions
examples.description: Iceland operates strict ITQ (Individual Transferable Quota) system under Fisheries Management Act. Annual TAC (Total Allowable Catch) set by Marine & Freshwater Research Institute. Quota year runs Sep-Aug. Quota can be exhausted early for popular species (cod), halting supply abruptly. Quota trading between vessel owners adds price volatility.
examples.typicalDelay: 2-8 weeks (until quota year reset or quota purchased)
examples.seasonality: Jun-Aug highest risk (quota exhaustion before Sep reset)
examples.rootCause: Sustainable fisheries management; fixed annual quotas; variable catch rates; quota concentration among large operators
examples.mitigationStrategy: Multi-species sourcing (cod + haddock + salmon); forward quota purchase agreements; diversify to Faroese/Norwegian backup suppliers
examples.workaroundDescription: Switch to alternative species if primary quota exhausted; source from Faroe Islands or Norway via same Samskip service

examples.name: BCP Inspection Delay at Immingham
examples.description: UK BTOM physical inspection rates for Icelandic fishery products run 5-15%. Immingham BCP is smaller than Felixstowe — limited reefer holding capacity. Lab turnaround for histamine, heavy metals, parasites 1-3 days. For fresh fish with 10-14 day product life, even 1-day delay is critical.
examples.seasonality: Worse Dec-Jan (holiday staffing) and bank holiday periods
examples.rootCause: BTOM inspection regime; limited Humber BCP capacity; lab resource constraints; fresh fish time sensitivity
examples.mitigationStrategy: Pre-arrival documentation excellence; maintain clean inspection history; submit IPAFFS 4+ days ahead; target mid-week arrivals

examples.name: Fresh Fish Shelf Life Pressure
examples.description: Fresh/chilled Icelandic fish has 10-14 day product life from processing. Transit consumes 4-7 days. Leaves 3-10 days for UK distribution, which is extremely tight. Any delay compounds: +1 day transit = -1 day retail window. UK supermarkets require minimum 5 days at intake. Below 3 days remaining: rejected or diverted to food service at 40-60% discount.
examples.typicalDelay: No delay — but any delay immediately erodes saleable window
examples.seasonality: Worse Oct-Mar when weather delays coincide
examples.rootCause: Inherent short shelf life of fresh fish; North Atlantic transit variability; no buffer for delays
examples.mitigationStrategy: Dual-temperature strategy: 60% frozen (no shelf-life pressure), 40% fresh (premium price justifies risk); MAP packaging for +3 day extension; air freight backup
examples.workaroundDescription: Convert order from fresh to frozen mid-chain if delay detected; air freight critical fresh orders at premium

examples.name: Air Freight Capacity Constraints (Keflavik)
examples.description: Air freight is the backup for urgent fresh fish when sea transit delayed. Keflavik has limited cargo capacity: Icelandair Cargo + occasional charter. Peak demand Dec (Christmas seafood) and Mar-Apr (Lent). Capacity fills quickly — 48-hour booking window. Cost 4-6x sea freight (ISK 800-1,200/kg vs ISK 200/kg sea).
examples.rootCause: Limited Keflavik cargo flight frequency; seasonal demand concentration; small island economy = limited aircraft
examples.mitigationStrategy: Standing air freight agreement with Icelandair Cargo; maintain cargo-ready packaging capability; pre-book Christmas allocation by October
examples.workaroundDescription: Charter cargo flight for high-value urgent consignments (break-even at 8+ tonnes); route via Copenhagen (CPH) as European hub with daily UK connections

examples.name: EEA/EFTA Regulatory Divergence from EU Post-Brexit
examples.description: Iceland's fishery export standards align with EU via EEA Agreement. Post-Brexit UK has introduced BTOM with different SPS requirements from EU. UK health cert format differs from EU model. Dual compliance burden for Icelandic exporters (EU format for EU, UK format for UK). MAST must issue separate UK-specific health certificates. Risk of regulatory drift increasing compliance cost and complexity.
examples.rootCause: Brexit regulatory divergence; UK BTOM evolution; EEA Agreement does not automatically extend to UK post-Brexit
examples.mitigationStrategy: Dedicated UK compliance officer at MAST; dual-format document templates; monitor UK DEFRA consultations for further changes

examples.cascadeEffect: Fresh fish shelf-life erosion; potential retail rejection if arrival <5 days remaining; forced diversion to foodservice at 40-60% discount
Supply gap forces customer to source from Norway/Faroe at higher price; potential customer defection; WWG unable to fulfil committed orders

examples.regulatoryConsequence: Increased future inspection frequency if non-conformance found
examples.cascadeEffect: Fresh fish shelf-life breach; demurrage; cold store booking missed; retailer delivery delayed
Total loss of fresh consignment; insurance claim; retailer confidence damaged; increased frozen ratio reduces margin
Margin compression from 15% to 2-5% on air-freighted consignment; if frequent, corridor economics become unviable for fresh
Dual compliance cost (EU + UK) erodes margin; smaller Icelandic exporters may exit UK market, reducing supply diversity

examples.statement: Icelandic seafood shipped Reykjavik to Immingham will arrive within 5 days of dispatch 85% of the time in Apr-Sep (summer), but only 65% of the time in Oct-Mar (winter), with winter P95 extending to 7 days
examples.baselineValue: Historical average 4.2 days (Apr-Sep 2023-2025, n=92) vs 5.8 days (Oct-Mar 2023-2025, n=88)
examples.predictedValue: Summer: 4 days median, 5 days P85. Winter: 5.5 days median, 7 days P95
examples.confidenceInterval: Summer 3-5 days (95% CI), Winter 4-7 days (95% CI)
examples.measurementMethod: Container GPS tracking gate-out Reykjavik to gate-in Immingham
examples.dataSource: Samskip container tracking API + WWG booking records 2023-2025
examples.actualOutcome: Iteration 2 (2024-25 winter): 62% within 5 days (slightly below 65% target); 3 shipments exceeded 7 days (Storm Eowyn)
examples.learningNote: Winter variability higher than modelled. Storm events cluster — when one delay occurs, subsequent sailings also affected by vessel schedule disruption.
examples.nextIteration: Add storm event frequency weighting; model carrier schedule reliability separately for Samskip vs Eimskip

examples.statement: Fresh chilled Icelandic cod fillets will arrive at Grimsby cold store with minimum 5 days remaining shelf life 80% of the time, with MAP-packed product achieving 7+ days remaining
examples.baselineValue: 12-day total shelf life (standard vacuum-pack); 15-day with MAP. Transit 4-7 days = 5-11 days consumed.
examples.predictedValue: Standard: 6 days remaining (summer), 4 days remaining (winter). MAP: 9 days remaining (summer), 7 days remaining (winter).
examples.confidenceInterval: Standard: 3-8 days remaining. MAP: 6-11 days remaining (95% CI).
examples.measurementMethod: Shelf-life = processing date + product life - arrival date at Grimsby DC
examples.dataSource: Thorbjorn dispatch records + WWG Grimsby intake records + temperature log correlation
examples.actualOutcome: 2025 pilot year: mean 5.2 days remaining (standard); MAP not yet trialled at scale
examples.variance: Within range but tight — 22% of standard-pack shipments arrived with <5 days
examples.learningNote: Standard vacuum-pack too risky for winter sailings. MAP packaging adds GBP 0.30/kg but extends window by 3 days — break-even if it avoids 1 rejection per 10 shipments.
examples.nextIteration: Trial MAP for all winter fresh consignments (Oct-Mar 2026-27); measure rejection rate reduction vs packaging cost increase

examples.statement: MAST health certificate issuance will be completed within 24 hours of application for 90% of standard consignments, with the remaining 10% requiring up to 3 business days due to laboratory testing or quota verification
examples.baselineValue: Current MAST SLA: 2-3 business days. Actual: 85% within 24 hours (2025 data, n=180).
examples.predictedValue: 90% within 24 hours by mid-2026 (MAST digital transformation programme)
examples.measurementMethod: Time from MAST application submission to signed health certificate available in TRACES system
examples.dataSource: MAST processing statistics + Thorbjorn application records
examples.learningNote: MAST is digitising certificate issuance (new online portal Q2 2026). Expected to reduce turnaround for standard consignments. Heavy metal lab testing for large cod still adds 2-3 days.

examples.name: Severe North Atlantic Storm Season — Multiple Sailing Cancellations
examples.triggerCondition: 3+ consecutive weeks of Force 9+ gales in North Atlantic (Oct-Mar); Samskip cancels 4+ sailings in 6-week period
examples.assumptions: Air freight partially available; frozen stock buffer exists; Eimskip also affected (same route)
examples.transitTimeChange: +3-7 days per shipment; cumulative 2-4 week supply gap
examples.costChange: +GBP 8,000-15,000 per container (air freight premium for critical fresh orders); +GBP 2,000 demurrage for delayed sea containers
examples.shelfLifeImpact: Fresh fish: total loss for delayed containers. Frozen: unaffected.
examples.mitigationPlan: Activate air freight for top 20% value fresh orders; draw down frozen buffer stock; notify customers of 2-week fresh fish suspension; source Norwegian backup if >3 weeks disrupted
examples.predictiveSignals: Met Office North Atlantic seasonal outlook; Samskip schedule reliability dropping below 70%; NAO (North Atlantic Oscillation) index strongly negative

examples.name: Iceland Cod Quota Cut — 20% Reduction
examples.triggerCondition: Marine & Freshwater Research Institute recommends 20%+ reduction in cod TAC for next quota year; Fisheries Ministry implements in September
examples.assumptions: Haddock and salmon quotas maintained; Norway and Faroe quotas stable; UK demand unchanged
examples.transitTimeChange: No transit change — but supply volume reduced 20%
examples.costChange: +25-40% raw material cost (ISK/kg cod); total landed cost +15-20%
examples.capacityChange: -20% cod volume; partial substitution with haddock
examples.demandImpact: UK demand maintained — price increase passed to customer or margin absorbed
examples.mitigationPlan: Diversify species mix (increase haddock/salmon share); secure forward quota purchase before announcement; activate Norwegian cod backup via Samskip Bergen service; negotiate customer price adjustment clauses
examples.predictiveSignals: Marine Research Institute stock assessment reports (published May-Jun); fishing industry lobby activity; historical quota trend analysis; cod spawning survey results

examples.name: UK Post-Brexit Seafood Inspection Rate Increase
examples.triggerCondition: UK DEFRA announces BTOM Phase 3 with increased SPS inspection rates for non-EU fishery products: physical checks from 5% to 20%
examples.assumptions: Applies to all non-EU fishery products equally; Immingham BCP capacity not expanded; inspection turnaround unchanged
examples.transitTimeChange: +0.5-2 days average (statistical: 20% of shipments delayed 1-3 days vs previous 5%)
examples.costChange: +GBP 1,500-3,000 per inspected consignment in BCP fees, demurrage, and shelf-life loss
examples.shelfLifeImpact: Fresh fish: -1-3 days for inspected consignments; ~4% of fresh shipments may breach minimum shelf life
examples.mitigationPlan: Invest in pre-shipment testing (every batch, not sample); lobby via Icelandic Fisheries Association; increase frozen:fresh ratio to 70:30; build 1-day buffer into all fresh product life calculations
examples.predictiveSignals: DEFRA consultation documents; parliamentary food safety committee hearings; APHA BCP performance data; non-conformance trends across all non-EU seafood imports

examples.name: Weather Delay leads to Fresh Fish Shelf Life Crisis
examples.description: North Atlantic weather delay of even 1-2 days pushes fresh fish below UK retailer minimum intake requirements (5 days remaining). Cascade: storm delay → late arrival → shelf life <5 days → retailer rejection → divert to foodservice at 40-60% discount → margin wiped → air freight activated for next shipment at 4-6x cost → corridor economics for fresh become unviable if repeated. Fresh fish is uniquely vulnerable because the product life (10-14 days) barely exceeds the transit time (4-7 days).
examples.triggerDelay: Immediate — every hour of weather delay erodes shelf life
examples.affectedParties: WWG importer (bears rejection cost + disposal); Icelandic exporter (reputation, future orders); retailer (out-of-stock on fresh fish counter)
examples.preventionAction: MAP packaging for all fresh; air freight backup pre-agreed; frozen buffer stock; convert fresh → frozen at origin if delay >24hrs forecast

examples.name: Quota Cut leads to Price Spike and Customer Impact
examples.description: Icelandic cod quota reduction triggers immediate raw material price increase (ISK/kg). Cascade: quota cut announced → spot market price +25-40% within weeks → forward contracts honoured at old price (margin loss) → new contracts at higher price → UK customers resist price increase → some customers switch to Norwegian or farmed cod → volume drops → fixed costs spread over fewer units → margin compression worsens. Small and medium Icelandic processors most vulnerable.
examples.triggerDelay: 2-4 weeks (market price adjustment) then 2-3 months (contract renegotiation)
examples.affectedParties: Icelandic processor (margin compression, may breach forward contracts); WWG importer (higher raw material cost, customer pushback); UK retailers (shelf price increase or range reduction)
examples.preventionAction: Multi-species diversification; forward quota purchase; customer price adjustment clauses; Norwegian backup supply agreements

examples.name: Inspection Increase leads to Fresh Product Rejection Wave
examples.description: Increased UK inspection rate (5% → 20%) means 4x more fresh fish consignments physically inspected. Fresh fish cannot tolerate even 1-day inspection delay. Cascade: more inspections → more delays → more fresh consignments breach shelf life → higher rejection rate → APHA records increased non-conformance → inspection rate increases further (vicious cycle) → fresh Icelandic fish becomes commercially unviable at >30% inspection rate. The regulatory system designed for frozen products penalises fresh disproportionately.
examples.triggerDelay: Immediate on implementation; vicious cycle develops over 3-6 months
examples.affectedParties: All Icelandic fresh fish exporters to UK; UK fresh fish importers; UK consumers (reduced fresh fish availability and higher prices)
examples.timeToRecover: 6-12 months (requires regulatory review or trusted trader programme)
examples.preventionAction: Lobby for time-sensitive product fast-track at BCP; invest in pre-departure testing (100% instead of sample); shift corridor to >60% frozen; negotiate BCP pre-clearance for compliant exporters

examples.name: Air Freight Cost Escalation leads to Corridor Viability Threat
examples.description: When sea freight fails (weather, delays), air freight is activated at 4-6x cost. If air freight used >15% of fresh consignments, the fresh corridor margin turns negative. Cascade: repeated sea delays → frequent air freight activation → annual air freight bill exceeds GBP 90,000 → fresh corridor contribution turns negative → pressure to abandon fresh entirely → lose premium pricing and customer relationships → corridor shrinks to frozen-only → lower margin, commodity competition with Russia/China.
examples.triggerDelay: Cumulative over 6-12 months (financial analysis reveals trend)
examples.affectedParties: WWG (margin erosion, strategic corridor viability); Icelandic processor (loss of premium fresh market); UK customers (reduced fresh availability)
examples.timeToRecover: Next summer season (Apr-Sep) when sea reliability recovers
examples.preventionAction: Cap air freight at 10% of fresh consignments; seasonal corridor strategy (fresh Apr-Sep only, frozen Oct-Mar); MAP investment to extend sea-viable window; negotiate customer shared air freight premium

examples.name: North Atlantic Storm Season (Oct-Mar)
examples.description: North Atlantic storms follow predictable seasonal pattern. Peak severity Dec-Feb. NAO (North Atlantic Oscillation) index predicts storm track: strongly negative NAO = storms track south across IS-UK route. Average 4-8 significant disruption events per winter. Correlation with Icelandic low-pressure system frequency.
examples.leadIndicators: NAO index forecast (NOAA, 10-14 day lead); Met Office North Atlantic weather warnings; Samskip/Eimskip schedule reliability index; Icelandic Met Office (Vedurstofa) 5-day forecast
examples.typicalImpact: 1-3 day vessel delay per event; 2-5 events per winter cause fresh fish shelf-life breach; cumulative 10-20 day supply disruption per winter
examples.recommendedAction: Monitor NAO index weekly Oct-Mar; pre-agree air freight standby with Icelandair Cargo by September; shift fresh:frozen ratio to 30:70 in winter; build 2-week frozen buffer before October

examples.name: Icelandic Fishing Quota Cycle (Annual Sep-Aug)
examples.description: Quota year runs Sep-Aug. TAC announcements May-Jun based on Marine Research Institute stock assessments. Quota exhaustion risk highest Jun-Aug. Cod quota has been stable 2020-2025 but haddock volatile. Quota trading prices peak Mar-May. Price signal: ISK/kg cod increases 10-20% when remaining quota <20%.
examples.leadIndicators: Marine Research Institute stock assessment (published May); remaining quota percentage (Directorate of Fisheries dashboard); quota trading price trend; ISK/kg spot price acceleration
examples.typicalImpact: Quota tight years: 10-15% price increase, 2-4 week supply gaps for specific species; quota stable years: predictable supply and pricing
examples.recommendedAction: Monitor Marine Research Institute publications from April; forward purchase quota before TAC announcement; diversify species to hedge single-species quota risk; maintain Norwegian backup supplier relationship

examples.name: UK Seafood Demand Peaks (Lent, Fish Friday, Christmas)
examples.description: UK seafood demand follows religious and cultural calendar. Lent (Feb-Apr) +15-25% for white fish. Fish Friday (year-round) creates weekly spike. Christmas +10-15% for premium seafood. Summer BBQ has minimal seafood uplift (unlike beef). Fish & chips shops create base demand floor. Frozen cod/haddock demand stable; fresh fish premium demand more volatile.
examples.observedFrequency: Annually — Lent peak + Christmas peak + weekly Fish Friday
examples.leadIndicators: Lent dates (published years ahead); retailer forward order volumes 8-12 weeks ahead; Seafish UK market reports; Easter date variability
examples.typicalImpact: Lent: volume +15-25%, fresh premium +10-15%; Christmas: volume +10-15%, premium species demand surge; Fish Friday: +20% fresh counter sales vs other weekdays
examples.recommendedAction: Pre-build frozen buffer 4 weeks before Lent; ensure air freight standby for Lent fresh surge; book Grimsby cold store Christmas allocation by October; plan fishing/processing schedule around Lent timing

examples.commodity: Fresh chilled Icelandic cod fillets — skinless, pin-boned, vacuum-packed
examples.quantity: 8 tonnes (1 × 40ft HC reefer — mixed: 5t fresh cod fillets + 3t frozen haddock portions)
examples.shelfLifeRemaining: 6 days (fresh cod at Grimsby intake); frozen haddock: 14 months
